title: Acute Left Middle Cerebral Artery Ischemic Stroke in 74-Year-Old Man with NIHSS 18 + Large Vessel Occlusion on CTA Within 90 Minutes of Last Known Well: Code Stroke Team Activation, IV Alteplase Within Door-to-Needle 60 Minutes, Emergent Endovascular Thrombectomy Within Door-to-Puncture 90 Minutes, Dysphagia Screening Before Any Oral Intake, and Multidisciplinary Post-Stroke Care
subtitle: ACUTE ISCHEMIC STROKE = brain tissue ischemia from arterial occlusion (atherosclerotic + cardioembolic + small vessel + cryptogenic + unusual causes) — time-critical management + every minute = ~1.9 million neurons lost; CODE STROKE TEAM ACTIVATION at recognition + multidisciplinary (ED + neurology + neurointervention + neurosurgery + radiology + critical care + nursing + pharmacy + lab + family liaison) within 10 minutes of arrival; NIHSS (NIH Stroke Scale) standardized assessment 0-42 (consciousness + orientation + commands + gaze + visual fields + facial palsy + arm + leg motor + ataxia + sensation + language + dysarthria + extinction); higher score = larger stroke + more severe + worse prognosis; NIHSS ≥6 indicates significant stroke; ≥10 large vessel occlusion likely + thrombectomy candidate; CT HEAD non-contrast IMMEDIATE to rule out hemorrhage + assess early ischemic changes (ASPECTS score 10-point assessment of middle cerebral artery territory; ≥6 favorable for treatment); CTA head + neck to assess for LARGE VESSEL OCCLUSION (LVO — ICA + M1 + M2 + basilar + others); CT PERFUSION (CTP) for extended-window selection (6-24 hr) — assess CORE INFARCT (irreversible) vs PENUMBRA (salvageable tissue with reperfusion); MRI selected cases; IV ALTEPLASE (tPA) within 4.5 HR of LKW (Last Known Well) per AHA/ASA guidelines + Door-to-Needle (DTN) ≤60 minutes goal (≤45 if achievable); DOSE 0.9 mg/kg max 90 mg = 10% IV bolus over 1 min + 90% infusion over 60 min; ADDITIONAL INDICATIONS post-2018 for extended window 4.5-9 hr in select patients with perfusion imaging mismatch (EXTEND + ECASS-4 trials); ALTERNATIVE TENECTEPLASE 0.25 mg/kg IV bolus (single dose) emerging as comparable or superior + simpler administration (EXTEND-IA TNK trials); CONTRAINDICATIONS to tPA include intracranial hemorrhage on CT + active internal bleeding + recent major surgery (<14 days) + recent stroke or head trauma (<3 months) + brain tumor + AVM + aneurysm + bleeding diathesis + INR >1.7 + platelets <100k + recent anticoagulation (DOAC within 48 hr or warfarin INR >1.7); SBP >185/110 (must control before tPA); RELATIVE contraindications also include recent surgery + GI/GU bleed + post-MI; ENDOVASCULAR THROMBECTOMY (mechanical clot retrieval) for LVO within 24 hr per AHA + multiple trials (DAWN + DEFUSE-3); Door-to-Puncture (DTP) ≤90 minutes goal; can be performed in addition to IV tPA (bridging therapy) or alone if tPA contraindicated; significantly improves outcomes for LVO; POST-tPA management = ICU level care × 24 hr + BP control (SBP <180/105 during 24 hr post-tPA to reduce hemorrhagic transformation risk) + neurochecks q15 min × 2 hr → q30 min × 6 hr → q1h × 16 hr; NO antiplatelet/anticoagulation × 24 hr post-tPA; repeat CT head at 24 hr post-tPA before resuming antithrombotic; HEMORRHAGIC TRANSFORMATION ~5-10% risk post-tPA (symptomatic ICH ~5%); MULTIDISCIPLINARY POST-STROKE CARE = dysphagia screening BEFORE any oral intake (NPO until swallow evaluation passed; aspiration pneumonia common + preventable); DVT prophylaxis (mechanical SCDs initially + heparin SC when bleeding risk acceptable); early mobility + PT/OT/speech therapy + cognitive rehabilitation; SECONDARY PREVENTION — DAPT (dual antiplatelet 90 days then aspirin alone for non-cardioembolic; DAPT trials POINT + CHANCE); ANTICOAGULATION for AFib (DOAC preferred — apixaban/rivaroxaban/dabigatran/edoxaban); STATIN high-intensity for atherosclerotic + LDL <70 target; BP optimization + lifestyle + diabetes management + smoking cessation + carotid stenting/endarterectomy if applicable; STROKE REHABILITATION specialized facility + intensive PT/OT/speech + cognitive + return to function + family + community + driving + employment + multidisciplinary lifelong

labs.n: PRESENTATION + LKW + stroke recognition + Code Stroke activation
labs.v: 74 y/o male J. Reynolds, retired engineer × 8 yr, brought to ED at 0820 today (Last Known Well 0700 — wife saw him reading newspaper + having coffee) after wife found him UNABLE TO SPEAK + RIGHT-SIDED WEAKNESS during breakfast preparation at 0750. PMH: HTN × 15 years (on amlodipine 10 mg daily — partially controlled); hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 20 mg daily — moderate intensity); type 2 diabetes × 10 years (metformin 1000 mg BID + HbA1c 7.4); ATRIAL FIBRILLATION first diagnosed today on admission ECG (NEW finding; not previously documented; possible source of cardioembolic stroke); chronic stable angina (occasional sublingual nitroglycerin); chronic LBP; mild cognitive impairment per recent neuropsych assessment 1 yr ago (donepezil 5 mg daily — early Alzheimer disease in clinical workup; baseline MMSE 26/30 stable). SOCIAL: married 50 years to wife (72, retired RN — recognizes stroke symptoms + immediately called 911); 3 adult children (all nearby); 6 grandchildren; community engaged (church + golf + bridge club); insurance excellent (Medicare + supplement); cultural background Irish-American + Catholic. PRESENTING ILLNESS: 0700 LKW = wife saw him reading newspaper + having coffee + normal speech + appearance; 0750 wife saw him during breakfast preparation unable to speak + R-sided weakness + facial droop + drooling + appeared confused — wife immediately called 911 (recognized stroke symptoms — BE FAST mnemonic: Balance + Eyes + Face + Arms + Speech + Time); EMS arrival 0805 = stroke recognized + BP 184/102 + glucose 142 + NIHSS estimated 18 (eye deviation + L gaze preference + R hemiparesis + R hemianopsia + global aphasia + R facial droop + dysarthria); EMS notification to receiving hospital + pre-arrival CODE STROKE activation. ED ARRIVAL 0820 (80 min from LKW; within 4.5 hr tPA window). HISTORY confirmed: NO recent surgery + NO recent trauma + NO recent hemorrhage + NO bleeding diathesis + NO recent anticoagulation use; warfarin or DOAC not on home medications; INR normal pending; not on antiplatelets either (was on aspirin 81 mg daily but stopped 2 weeks ago after dental procedure + not restarted); current medications = amlodipine 10 mg daily + atorvastatin 20 mg daily + metformin 1000 mg BID + donepezil 5 mg daily + occasional sublingual nitroglycerin + sumatriptan PRN. ED ASSESSMENT confirmed NIHSS 18 — severe stroke + LIKELY LARGE VESSEL OCCLUSION + thrombectomy candidate; BP 192/108 elevated (needs lowering for tPA eligibility — must be <185/110 before tPA); HR 94 sinus + irregular consistent with AFib (NEW finding on admission ECG = AFib with rapid ventricular response 110-130 average); NIH STROKE SCALE 18 components: alert/can't speak/follows one command/forced L gaze/R hemianopia/R facial droop/L extremities normal/R arm 0/5/R leg 0/5/can't assess ataxia in plegic/R sensory loss/global aphasia/severe dysarthria/no extinction; weight 80 kg estimated; PUPILS equal + reactive; CARDIOPULMONARY exam normal; ABDOMEN normal; INTUBATED PHARYNX patent. CODE STROKE TEAM ACTIVATED at 0820 with multidisciplinary response: NEUROLOGIST (Dr. Lopez) + NEUROINTERVENTIONALIST (Dr. Park) + EMERGENCY MD + STROKE COORDINATOR + ICU nurse + radiology tech + pharmacy + scribe + lab + family liaison; family (wife + daughter en route) notified.
labs.r: ACUTE ISCHEMIC STROKE = time-critical neurological emergency requiring CODE STROKE TEAM ACTIVATION + multidisciplinary response + rapid assessment + imaging + treatment within tight windows (tPA <4.5 hr LKW; thrombectomy <24 hr LKW for LVO). RECOGNITION + ACTIVATION + STAT WORKUP foundational. RN role: rapid assessment + IV access + labs + BP control + family communication + multidisciplinary coordination.

labs.n: NIH STROKE SCALE + STROKE WORKUP + imaging + tPA decision
labs.v: NIH STROKE SCALE (NIHSS) = standardized stroke severity assessment 0-42 (higher = more severe + worse prognosis); 11 components: (1a) LEVEL OF CONSCIOUSNESS 0-3 (alert → coma); (1b) LOC ORIENTATION questions 0-2; (1c) LOC COMMANDS 0-2; (2) BEST GAZE 0-2 (forced deviation); (3) VISUAL FIELDS 0-3 (homonymous hemianopia); (4) FACIAL PALSY 0-3 (minor → complete); (5a) MOTOR ARM L 0-4 (no drift → no movement); (5b) MOTOR ARM R 0-4; (6a) MOTOR LEG L 0-4; (6b) MOTOR LEG R 0-4; (7) LIMB ATAXIA 0-2; (8) SENSORY 0-2 (mild → severe); (9) BEST LANGUAGE 0-3 (aphasia); (10) DYSARTHRIA 0-2; (11) EXTINCTION + INATTENTION 0-2; max total 42. SEVERITY: 0-4 mild + 5-15 moderate + 16-20 moderate-severe + 21+ severe; NIHSS ≥6 indicates significant stroke; ≥10 large vessel occlusion likely + thrombectomy candidate. THIS PATIENT NIHSS 18 = moderate-severe + LVO likely + thrombectomy candidate + significant outcome benefit if reperfusion achieved. STAT NEUROIMAGING per AHA stroke guidelines: (1) CT HEAD NON-CONTRAST = first imaging; rule out ACUTE INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE (absolute contraindication to tPA + alternative management); assess EARLY ISCHEMIC CHANGES (loss of gray-white differentiation + hypoattenuation + sulcal effacement + insular ribbon sign + hyperdense MCA sign + lentiform nucleus obscuration); ASPECTS (Alberta Stroke Program Early CT Score) 10-point assessment of MCA territory — scores ≥6 favorable for treatment + <6 less favorable (large core infarct + lower benefit + higher hemorrhage risk); THIS PATIENT ASPECTS 8/10 favorable; (2) CTA HEAD + NECK = vascular imaging; identifies LARGE VESSEL OCCLUSION (LVO) — ICA + M1 + M2 + basilar + others; required for thrombectomy planning; can also evaluate carotid + vertebral atherosclerosis + Circle of Willis; (3) CT PERFUSION (CTP) = identifies CORE INFARCT (irreversibly damaged tissue; CBV/CBF severely reduced) vs PENUMBRA (salvageable tissue with reperfusion — peripheral oligemia + hypoperfusion + delayed time-to-peak); allows EXTENDED-WINDOW selection (6-24 hr post-LKW) for thrombectomy per DAWN + DEFUSE-3 trials; favorable = small core + large penumbra (mismatch ≥1.8 + core <70 mL); THIS PATIENT favorable CTP (core 30 mL + penumbra 180 mL = mismatch ratio 6 = thrombectomy benefit anticipated); (4) MRI selected cases (more sensitive for acute infarct + posterior fossa + cervical dissection + nontraditional presentations) — diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) shows acute infarct earliest. tPA (IV ALTEPLASE) CRITERIA per AHA/ASA: ELIGIBLE = (1) clinical diagnosis of ischemic stroke; (2) measurable neurologic deficit; (3) symptom onset clearly defined + WITHIN 4.5 HR of LKW (LAST KNOWN WELL); (4) age 18+; (5) blood pressure <185/110 (treat to lower if needed); (6) no acute intracranial hemorrhage on CT; (7) no large established infarct on CT (ASPECTS ≥6); (8) no recent major surgery (<14 days) + no recent stroke (<3 months) + no recent head trauma (<3 months) + no brain tumor + no AVM + no aneurysm; (9) coagulation status acceptable (INR <1.7 + platelets >100k + no recent therapeutic anticoagulant exposure); (10) glucose >50 + no recent GI/GU bleed + multiple other specific contraindications. EXTENDED-WINDOW tPA 4.5-9 HR per EXTEND/ECASS-4 in selected patients with FAVORABLE PERFUSION IMAGING MISMATCH (DWI vs PWI mismatch on MRI or CTP); newer evidence supports treating wake-up stroke or LKW unclear if perfusion imaging favorable. ALTERNATIVE TENECTEPLASE 0.25 mg/kg IV bolus (single dose) emerging as comparable or superior to alteplase per EXTEND-IA TNK + multiple trials — simpler administration (no infusion) + may improve outcomes; gaining acceptance + some institutions now use as first-line. ALTEPLASE DOSING per AHA: 0.9 mg/kg IV (max 90 mg) = 10% IV BOLUS over 1 min + 90% INFUSION over 60 min; THIS PATIENT 80 kg × 0.9 = 72 mg = 7.2 mg bolus + 64.8 mg over 60 min. THIS PATIENT met all criteria + tPA initiated within DOOR-TO-NEEDLE 40 minutes (within ≤60 min goal; some centers achieve ≤45 or ≤30 with optimized workflow).
labs.r: NIHSS standardized assessment + STAT neuroimaging (CT head + CTA + CTP) + tPA criteria/contraindications + Door-to-Needle time critical. TENECTEPLASE emerging alternative. EXTENDED-WINDOW tPA 4.5-9 hr selected with perfusion imaging. RN role: NIHSS assessment + imaging coordination + medication administration + monitoring + family + multidisciplinary.

labs.v: BLOOD PRESSURE management for tPA eligibility + safety: (1) PRE-tPA TARGET = SBP <185 + DBP <110 (must be controlled before tPA initiation; sustained higher pressures contraindicate tPA — increased hemorrhagic transformation risk); (2) IV ANTIHYPERTENSIVES preferred (rapid onset + titratable): LABETALOL 10-20 mg IV bolus over 1-2 min (can repeat every 10-20 min; max 300 mg cumulative; CONTRAINDICATIONS = asthma + severe HF + heart block); NICARDIPINE 5 mg/hr IV infusion + titrate by 2.5 mg/hr q5-15 min (max 15 mg/hr; vasodilator + smooth response); CLEVIDIPINE 1-2 mg/hr titrated (newer + similar to nicardipine + may have faster onset/offset); HYDRALAZINE 10 mg IV q4-6h PRN (alternative + reflex tachycardia); ENALAPRILAT 0.625-1.25 mg IV (alternative + ACE inhibitor). AVOID rapid drops (more than 20% reduction in MAP can extend penumbra into infarct); REASSESS q15 min during titration; (3) POST-tPA TARGET first 24 hr = SBP <180 + DBP <105 (lower threshold to reduce hemorrhagic transformation risk; treat if exceeds + use IV antihypertensives + arterial line for continuous monitoring); after 24 hr post-tPA = standard BP goals (SBP <140 per recent evidence + AHA) + transition to oral antihypertensives. tPA ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATIONS per AHA: (1) acute intracranial hemorrhage on CT; (2) ischemic stroke or severe head trauma within 3 months; (3) acute MI within 3 months (relative); (4) intracranial neoplasm + AVM + aneurysm; (5) hypertension >185/110 unresponsive to treatment; (6) active internal bleeding (recent GI/GU bleed within 21 days; recent major surgery within 14 days); (7) bleeding diathesis (INR >1.7 + platelets <100k + recent therapeutic anticoagulant — DOAC within 48 hr or warfarin INR >1.7); (8) glucose <50 OR >400 (relative — extreme); (9) age <18; (10) extremely large established infarct (relative — ASPECTS <6 historically). RELATIVE contraindications include: NIHSS <4 or rapidly improving (consider benefit vs risk); pregnancy; recent MI; recent GI/GU bleed; recent surgery (<14 days minor + <3 months major); recent acute MI; chronic anticoagulant use (review timing + reversal); seizure at onset (need to distinguish from postictal); minor + non-disabling deficits; advanced age (no upper age limit per AHA — treat based on individual). HEMORRHAGIC TRANSFORMATION post-tPA ~5-10% incidence; SYMPTOMATIC INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE (sICH) ~5% (worsening neuro + new hemorrhage on CT); FATAL ICH ~1-2%; TREATMENT if sICH = STOP tPA + emergent CT head + neurosurgery consult + fresh frozen plasma + cryoprecipitate + reverse antifibrinolytic; SCREENING for sICH = neurochecks q15 min × 2 hr post-tPA then q30 min × 6 hr then q1h × 16 hr (24 hr total intensive monitoring per AHA + institutional protocols). DOOR-TO-NEEDLE (DTN) TIME goal ≤60 minutes per AHA (target stroke metrics) + ≤45 min achievable + ≤30 min optimal; faster = better outcomes. THIS PATIENT BP 192/108 on arrival → labetalol 10 mg IV → BP 172/96 → additional labetalol if needed → BP 168/92 controlled <185/110 → tPA initiated; Door-to-Needle 40 minutes (within target).
labs.r: BP management for tPA: <185/110 pre-tPA + <180/105 first 24 hr post-tPA. Labetalol + nicardipine + clevidipine + hydralazine + enalaprilat options. Avoid rapid drops. tPA absolute + relative contraindications. Hemorrhagic transformation ~5-10% + sICH ~5%. Door-to-Needle ≤60 min goal. RN role: BP monitoring + medication administration + recognition + escalation.

labs.v: ENDOVASCULAR THROMBECTOMY (mechanical clot retrieval) for LARGE VESSEL OCCLUSION (LVO) = transformative treatment + significantly improves outcomes; INDICATIONS per AHA/ASA: (1) anterior circulation LVO (ICA + M1 + selected M2 + selected proximal A1/A2); (2) NIHSS ≥6 (most patients; some institutions accept lower with LVO); (3) ASPECTS ≥6 favorable (lower may still benefit with extended workflow + careful selection); (4) age 18+ (no upper limit per AHA; elderly benefit + individualized); (5) modified Rankin Scale (mRS) ≤1 pre-stroke (functional baseline; many institutions accept higher with individual assessment); (6) Door-to-Puncture ≤90 minutes goal + Door-to-Reperfusion ≤30 min additional. EXTENDED WINDOW (DAWN + DEFUSE-3 trials): 6-24 HOURS from LKW for LVO if FAVORABLE PERFUSION IMAGING (small core + significant penumbra mismatch); these trials extended thrombectomy benefit beyond traditional 6 hr window. THROMBECTOMY DEVICES: STENT RETRIEVER (Solitaire + Trevo + others; deploy stent in clot + retract + retrieve clot in stent); ASPIRATION CATHETERS (large-bore catheters + suction + retrieve clot); COMBINATIONS for difficult cases; experienced neurointerventionalist + technique varies. TICI (Thrombolysis in Cerebral Infarction) SCORE for reperfusion success: 0 = no reperfusion; 1 = penetration without distal filling; 2a = partial filling <50%; 2b = partial filling ≥50%; 3 = complete reperfusion; TICI 2b/3 considered successful reperfusion + correlates with good outcomes. ANESTHESIA = GENERAL ANESTHESIA preferred in many centers (airway protection + immobility + cooperation + safe for neurointerventionalist); some centers use CONSCIOUS SEDATION for select patients; trials mixed on outcomes (general vs conscious — both acceptable; institutional + patient factors guide). MULTIDISCIPLINARY = neurointerventionalist + neuro anesthesia + ICU + neuro RN + radiology tech + scribe + family liaison. BRIDGING THERAPY = IV tPA + endovascular thrombectomy combined; both contribute to recanalization; tPA may dissolve some clot + facilitate retrieval + improve distal microvasculature; thrombectomy retrieves residual clot + restores flow; both indicated together for LVO when patient eligible for both. NEUROINTERVENTION POST-PROCEDURE = monitor neuro status + groin site (femoral access) + bleeding + neuro recovery + neurochecks per protocol + ICU monitoring × 24 hr. COMPLICATIONS = hemorrhagic transformation (similar incidence to tPA alone; 5-10%); vessel dissection or perforation; embolization to new territory; groin hematoma + pseudoaneurysm + arteriovenous fistula; contrast nephropathy; multidisciplinary management. POST-THROMBECTOMY outcomes: ~50-60% achieve good functional outcome (mRS 0-2) vs ~25-30% with tPA alone for LVO — significant improvement; THIS PATIENT NIHSS improved from 18 → 6 → 4 + dramatic clinical response indicating successful reperfusion + favorable trajectory.
labs.r: ENDOVASCULAR THROMBECTOMY for LVO = standard treatment + significantly improves outcomes vs tPA alone; Door-to-Puncture ≤90 min + extended-window 6-24 hr per DAWN/DEFUSE-3; bridging therapy (tPA + thrombectomy) common; TICI 2b/3 successful reperfusion. RN role: pre/post-procedure care + family + monitoring + escalation.

labs.v: POST-STROKE CARE comprehensive multidisciplinary approach: (1) ICU LEVEL CARE × 24 HR post-tPA + post-thrombectomy + continuous monitoring + arterial line + IV access + Foley + cardiac monitor + pulse oximetry; (2) NEUROCHECKS q15 min × 2 hr → q30 min × 6 hr → q1h × 16 hr per AHA post-tPA protocol; assess any change in level of consciousness + NIHSS + new focal deficit + signs of cerebral edema or hemorrhagic transformation (severe headache + N/V + Cushing triad + papilledema + worsening level of consciousness); EMERGENT CT HEAD if any concerning change; (3) BP CONTROL first 24 hr <180/105 (treat with IV antihypertensives); after 24 hr standard BP goals + transition to oral; (4) ANTITHROMBOTIC HOLD × 24 HR post-tPA — NO antiplatelets/anticoagulation until repeat CT head clear at 24 hr post-tPA; HALT all anticoagulants on admission + reverse if needed (FFP + PCC + vitamin K for warfarin reversal; idarucizumab for dabigatran; andexanet alfa for FXa inhibitors); (5) DYSPHAGIA SCREENING BEFORE ANY ORAL INTAKE — NPO until SLP swallow evaluation passed; aspiration pneumonia is common post-stroke + preventable; bedside swallow screen (water swallow test) initial; SLP formal evaluation; gradual diet advancement per evaluation (NPO → ice chips → puree → soft → regular); modified textures + thickened liquids if needed; nutrition support (NG/Dobhoff/PEG if persistent dysphagia + duration); (6) DVT PROPHYLAXIS — mechanical SCDs initially; LOW-DOSE HEPARIN 5000 units SC q8h or ENOXAPARIN 40 mg SC daily start at 24 hr post-tPA if no hemorrhagic transformation; reduces DVT + PE risk; (7) STRESS ULCER prophylaxis = pantoprazole 40 mg IV/PO daily; (8) SKIN PROTECTION + pressure injury prevention + Braden + q2h repositioning + pressure-redistribution mattress; (9) EARLY MOBILITY when safe + post-tPA + multidisciplinary PT/OT bedside + active assistance with ROM + transfers + ambulation; (10) BOWEL/BLADDER care + Foley assessment + reduce + bowel regimen + continence assessment + plan for retraining; (11) NUTRITION + dysphagia diet + adequate calories + protein + supplementation if needed + dietitian; (12) ASPIRATION PRECAUTIONS — HOB ≥30° + oral chlorhexidine + oral care q4h + supervised feeding + thin liquids if appropriate + thickened if dysphagia + suction available; (13) GLUCOSE management 140-180 (avoid extreme hyperglycemia which worsens stroke outcomes; avoid hypoglycemia); (14) FAMILY-CENTERED + cultural sensitivity + emotional support + chaplain + social worker. SECONDARY STROKE PREVENTION: (1) ANTIPLATELET vs ANTICOAGULATION per stroke etiology — for NON-CARDIOEMBOLIC small vessel/atherosclerotic stroke = ASPIRIN 81 mg daily + consider DAPT 90 days (aspirin + clopidogrel per CHANCE + POINT trials in minor stroke/TIA reduce recurrence) then aspirin alone; for CARDIOEMBOLIC from AFib = ANTICOAGULATION (DOAC preferred over warfarin per AHA + ARISTOTLE/RE-LY/ROCKET-AF/ENGAGE-AF/HOKUSAI-VTE — apixaban + dabigatran + rivaroxaban + edoxaban; reduces stroke 50%+ + reduces intracranial bleeding vs warfarin); apixaban 5 mg BID (or 2.5 mg BID if 2 of 3: age ≥80 + weight ≤60 kg + Cr ≥1.5; reduced dose); rivaroxaban 20 mg daily (or 15 mg if CrCl 15-50); dabigatran 150 mg BID (or 110 mg if age ≥75 + bleed risk); edoxaban 60 mg daily (or 30 mg if CrCl 15-50 + weight ≤60 kg + P-gp inhibitor); avoid in mechanical valves (use warfarin); start anticoagulation at 1-14 days post-stroke depending on infarct size + bleeding risk; THIS PATIENT = AFib new-onset + cardioembolic stroke + start apixaban 5 mg BID Day 2; (2) STATIN HIGH-INTENSITY = ATORVASTATIN 80 mg daily or ROSUVASTATIN 20-40 mg daily for atherosclerotic + LDL target <70 (SPARCL trial high-intensity statin reduces stroke recurrence; even with normal LDL in stroke); for cardioembolic strokes statin still recommended for atherosclerotic comorbidity; (3) BP OPTIMIZATION = SBP <140 (some target <130 in selected); lifestyle + ACE-I or ARB or thiazide or CCB combination; (4) DIABETES management + HbA1c <7-8 individualized; (5) LIFESTYLE = Mediterranean diet + exercise + weight management + smoking cessation + alcohol moderation; (6) CAROTID STENTING or ENDARTERECTOMY for symptomatic carotid stenosis (50-99% on side of stroke); (7) PFO (patent foramen ovale) closure for selected cryptogenic strokes; (8) SCREENING for sleep apnea + cardiovascular optimization. MULTIDISCIPLINARY POST-STROKE CARE = PT (physical therapy + gait + balance + strength + mobility) + OT (occupational therapy + ADLs + cognition + visual + perceptual) + SPEECH (language + swallowing + cognitive) + NEUROPSYCHOLOGY + RECREATIONAL + VOCATIONAL therapy + CASE MANAGEMENT + SOCIAL WORK + CHAPLAIN + family + REHABILITATION specialized (acute inpatient rehab if functional + skilled nursing facility + outpatient + home therapy + community); DRIVING evaluation when appropriate + employment planning + return to function + community reintegration; LIFELONG management + chronic disease + secondary prevention + family + community.
labs.r: POST-STROKE CARE = ICU 24 hr post-tPA + neurochecks + BP control + antithrombotic hold 24 hr + DYSPHAGIA SCREENING BEFORE ORAL INTAKE + DVT prophylaxis + nutrition + skin + early mobility + multidisciplinary rehab. SECONDARY PREVENTION = appropriate antiplatelet vs anticoagulation per etiology + statin + BP + diabetes + lifestyle + carotid + multidisciplinary lifelong management.

labs.n: LEADERSHIP + DELEGATION in Code Stroke + multidisciplinary

EMERGENCY MD or hospitalist (initial assessment + airway + IV access); ED RN or stroke RN (vital signs + IV access + medication preparation + family); NEUROINTERVENTIONALIST (interventional radiology + thrombectomy planning); LAB tech (STAT lab draw + processing); SCRIBE (real-time documentation + door-to-times); FAMILY LIAISON (CNS or chaplain — communicating with family + emotional support); RUNNER (UAP for transport + supplies); team huddle pre-imaging + decision-making + tPA + thrombectomy + transfer + post-event debriefing within 30 min + formal review at 24-48 hr + system improvement opportunities + just culture + EAP + peer support; INSTITUTIONAL stroke certification (Joint Commission Primary Stroke Center + Comprehensive Stroke Center + Thrombectomy-Capable Center) standardize processes + quality metrics; TELESTROKE for remote consultation + decision support in centers without on-site neurologist (telemedicine consultation + image transfer + remote NIHSS + treatment decision). labs.r: CODE STROKE TEAM ACTIVATION + LEADERSHIP + DELEGATION + multidisciplinary + closed-loop communication + parallel processing + standardized pathway = time-critical optimization. RN role: team activation + role execution + scribe + escalation + family + multidisciplinary.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES: NO acute intracranial hemorrhage on CT head (tPA + thrombectomy candidate); NO recent surgery + NO recent trauma + NO bleeding diathesis + NO recent anticoagulation use (no tPA contraindications); NO acute MI/arrhythmia beyond new AFib (cardiac monitoring + troponin pending; AFib is precipitant + treated post-stroke); NO acute respiratory failure beyond intubation for thrombectomy (extubated post-procedure); NO acute mental health crisis pre-stroke; NO acute renal failure or hepatic dysfunction; NO seizure activity; NO meningismus or signs of meningitis; insurance + multidisciplinary team available + experienced (Primary Stroke Center designation); family supportive + wife is RN (medical literacy + recognized symptoms + immediate 911 call). PROTECTIVE FACTORS: WIFE recognized stroke symptoms (BE FAST mnemonic) + IMMEDIATELY called 911 (knew stroke is time-critical from RN background; this single intervention saved time + improved outcomes); LAST KNOWN WELL clearly defined at 0700 (vs uncertain LKW which limits treatment options); WITHIN 4.5 HR tPA window + within 24 hr thrombectomy window; PROMPT EMS recognition + pre-arrival hospital notification + CODE STROKE activation; RAPID ED workflow + Door-to-Needle 40 minutes (within ≤60 min goal); MULTIDISCIPLINARY TEAM experienced + integrated; THROMBECTOMY-CAPABLE CENTER with neurointerventionalist + interventional radiology suite; LARGE VESSEL OCCLUSION amenable to thrombectomy + significant penumbra (favorable CTP) + successful TICI 2b/3 recanalization; FAMILY supportive + extended family available + insurance excellent + multidisciplinary outpatient care accessible; PATIENT pre-stroke functional baseline good (mRS likely 0-1) + decisional capacity intact pre-stroke + receptive to treatment + good prognosis with reperfusion; NEW AFib identification + appropriate anticoagulation for secondary prevention (apixaban) reduces recurrence 50%+; LIFESTYLE addressable (DM + HTN + hyperlipidemia + smoking management); cultural + spiritual support available (Catholic + chaplain) + family integration; PROMPT and successful reperfusion translates to dramatic NIHSS improvement 18 → 6 → 4 = excellent recovery trajectory.
labs.r: Despite severe initial stroke, multiple PROTECTIVE FACTORS supported excellent outcome: wife RN early recognition + immediate 911 + clear LKW + within treatment window + prompt EMS + Code Stroke + rapid workflow + experienced multidisciplinary team + thrombectomy-capable center + favorable imaging + successful reperfusion + family + insurance + good baseline. Standard ideal trajectory with comprehensive intervention + lifelong management.

nursesNote: 0820 — 74 y/o male J. Reynolds brought to ED via EMS at 0820 today (LAST KNOWN WELL 0700; 80 min from LKW; within 4.5 hr tPA window) after wife found him unable to speak + R-sided weakness during breakfast preparation at 0750. HISTORY per wife (72 y/o retired RN — recognized stroke symptoms + immediately called 911 — RN background was protective factor for early recognition): patient was reading newspaper + having coffee at 0700 (LKW = normal); at 0750 wife saw him at kitchen table unable to speak + R-sided weakness + facial droop + drooling + appeared confused; wife immediately called 911 (recognized BE FAST mnemonic — Balance + Eyes + Face + Arms + Speech + Time); EMS arrival 0805 + stroke recognized + BP 184/102 + glucose 142 + NIHSS estimated 18 + pre-arrival notification to ED at 0815. PMH: HTN × 15 yr (amlodipine 10 mg daily — partially controlled), hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 20 mg daily — moderate intensity), T2DM × 10 yr (metformin 1000 BID + HbA1c 7.4), ATRIAL FIBRILLATION first diagnosed today on admission ECG (NEW finding; not previously documented; possible source of CARDIOEMBOLIC stroke), chronic stable angina (occasional SL nitroglycerin), chronic LBP, mild cognitive impairment per neuropsych assessment 1 yr ago (donepezil 5 mg daily — early Alzheimer disease in clinical workup; baseline MMSE 26/30 stable). SOCIAL: married 50 yr to wife (72, retired RN — supportive + at bedside throughout); 3 adult children all nearby; 6 grandchildren; community engaged + Catholic + Irish-American + Catholic; insurance Medicare + supplement excellent. NO recent surgery + NO recent trauma + NO bleeding diathesis + NO recent anticoagulant use (was on aspirin 81 mg daily but stopped 2 weeks ago after dental procedure + not restarted; relevant — patient with new AFib not anticoagulated). ED ARRIVAL 0820 (80 min from LKW; within 4.5 hr tPA window). VITALS: HR 94 sinus + irregular = AFIB RVR (NEW finding; not previously documented); BP 192/108 MAP 136 (markedly elevated; needs lowering for tPA eligibility — must be <185/110 before tPA); RR 20; SpO2 96% RA; T 37.0°C; weight 80 kg estimated. NIH STROKE SCALE 18 — severe stroke + LIKELY LVO + thrombectomy candidate: alert/can't speak (aphasia)/follows one command/forced L gaze/R hemianopia/R facial droop/L extremities normal/R arm 0/5/R leg 0/5/can't assess ataxia/R sensory loss/global aphasia/severe dysarthria/no extinction = NIHSS 18. CODE STROKE TEAM ACTIVATED 0820 with multidisciplinary response within 10 minutes: TEAM LEADER NEUROLOGIST (Dr. Lopez); NEUROINTERVENTIONALIST (Dr. Park); EMERGENCY MD; STROKE COORDINATOR; STROKE RN; ICU RN; RADIOLOGY TECH; PHARMACY (tPA preparation); SCRIBE (real-time documentation + door-to-times); FAMILY LIAISON (CNS for wife + daughter en route); ROLE assignments in 30-60 seconds via closed-loop communication; PARALLEL PROCESSING (rather than sequential) — NIHSS + IV access + labs + BP control + imaging + tPA preparation + thrombectomy preparation simultaneously. STAT WORKUP completed in parallel: 2 large-bore IVs (16-gauge) + cardiac monitor + pulse ox + STAT LABS (CBC + BMP + LFT + lipase + lactate + ABG + coags PT/INR + PTT + fibrinogen + troponin + BNP + glucose + lipid panel + HbA1c + cultures); GLUCOSE 142 + INR 1.1 normal + platelets 220 + electrolytes normal + ECG = AFIB with rapid ventricular response 110-130 average (NEW finding; possible source of cardioembolic stroke); CT HEAD non-contrast at 0830 = NO acute hemorrhage + early ischemic changes in L MCA territory with ASPECTS 8/10 (favorable for treatment + small core); CTA HEAD + NECK at 0835 = LEFT M1 OCCLUSION (LARGE VESSEL OCCLUSION confirmed; thrombectomy candidate); CT PERFUSION at 0840 = ~30 mL core infarct + ~180 mL penumbra (mismatch ratio 6 = favorable for reperfusion + thrombectomy benefit anticipated). BP CONTROL initiated: LABETALOL 10 mg IV at 0830 + 0840 + 0850 (total 30 mg) → BP 172/96 → 168/92 (controlled <185/110 threshold required for tPA); metoprolol 5 mg IV at 0845 for AFib rate control (HR 110 → 92). tPA DECISION 0900 (40 min from arrival; within ≤60 min Door-to-Needle goal): contraindications reviewed — no acute hemorrhage + no recent surgery + no recent stroke + no brain tumor + no bleeding diathesis + INR 1.1 normal + platelets 220 normal + not on anticoagulant + BP controlled <185/110 + glucose normal + age 74 (no upper limit); CONSENT obtained from wife (healthcare proxy) + patient with limited communication (alphabet board + nodding); IV ALTEPLASE (tPA) 0.9 mg/kg × 80 kg = 72 mg → 10% IV BOLUS 7.2 mg over 1 min + 90% INFUSION 64.8 mg over 60 min initiated at 0900 (DOOR-TO-NEEDLE TIME 40 minutes; within ≤60 min goal); IV LR maintenance + cardiac + neuro monitoring + BP titration during tPA. ENDOVASCULAR THROMBECTOMY activated simultaneously by neurointerventionalist for LVO + appropriate imaging; Door-to-Puncture goal ≤90 minutes; interventional radiology suite prep + neuro anesthesia + multidisciplinary planning; transferred to IR suite at 1000 (end of tPA infusion). THROMBECTOMY 1000-1100 under general anesthesia: STENT RETRIEVER (Solitaire) + aspiration catheter used; LARGE M1 CLOT retrieved + small thrombus extending into M2; TICI 2b/3 recanalization achieved (good reperfusion); no immediate procedural complications; femoral access site secured; post-procedure CT head pending. Transferred to NEUROLOGY ICU (Stroke Unit) Bed 7 at 1130 + extubated as anesthesia wakes up. NEUROLOGY ICU 1200 (1 hr post-thrombectomy): alert + opens eyes + follows simple commands + R arm 4/5 (improved from 0/5 — significant recovery with reperfusion) + R leg 4/5 + speech improving + some words (improved from global aphasia) + facial droop persisting mild; NIHSS now 6 (DRAMATICALLY improved from 18 — significant clinical response to tPA + thrombectomy; reperfusion successful); CT head post-procedure shows reperfusion changes + no hemorrhagic transformation; ICU level care × 24 hr post-tPA per AHA protocol; BP target <180/105 first 24 hr post-tPA (continued nicardipine drip + transition to PO); NEUROCHECKS q15 min × 2 hr → q30 min × 6 hr → q1h × 16 hr per post-tPA protocol; family at bedside + tearful + relieved + grateful; chaplain visit + emotional support. DAY 2 morning 0700 (24 hr post-tPA): NIHSS now 4 (continuing improvement); facial droop mild + R arm 4+/5 + R leg 4+/5 + speech improved + dysarthria mild + comprehension good; CT head 24 hr post-tPA = NO hemorrhagic transformation + reperfusion changes only + small area of established infarct in L M1 territory (expected); DYSPHAGIA SCREENING completed by SLP at 0900 = PASSED with mild residue + safe for soft diet with thin liquids (NPO until passed — aspiration pneumonia common + preventable post-stroke); SECONDARY PREVENTION started: APIXABAN 5 mg PO BID (DOAC chosen for AFib — preferred over warfarin per AHA + ARISTOTLE trial; reduces stroke 50%+ + reduces intracranial bleeding vs warfarin; start anticoagulation Day 2 post-tPA based on infarct size + bleeding risk + AFib indication); ATORVASTATIN increased to 80 mg PO daily (high-intensity statin); LISINOPRIL 10 mg PO daily (BP optimization + secondary stroke prevention); aspirin 81 mg daily started Day 2 then transitioned to apixaban alone (no DAPT in this patient — cardioembolic stroke so apixaban alone preferred); PT/OT/SPEECH therapy initiated + intensive rehab + cognitive assessment; family at bedside + receptive + grateful + multidisciplinary engagement. PLAN: continue ICU monitoring × 24 hr → transition to stroke unit floor when stable; continue secondary prevention + apixaban + statin + BP optimization + diabetes management; intensive rehabilitation (acute inpatient rehab consideration based on functional status + progress); dysphagia ongoing surveillance + diet advancement + speech therapy; carotid evaluation (CTA already showed no significant carotid stenosis); cardiac echo (TTE + TEE if needed) for AFib + cardioembolic source; multidisciplinary discharge planning + outpatient follow-up + driving restrictions + family + community + cultural + spiritual support throughout + chaplain ongoing + Catholic family rituals respected.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this acute stroke presentation?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically + critical care significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: ACUTE LMCA ISCHEMIC STROKE with NIH STROKE SCALE 18 (severe stroke + LVO likely + thrombectomy candidate); LAST KNOWN WELL 0700 (80 min before ED arrival — WITHIN 4.5 HR tPA WINDOW); BE FAST recognition by wife (RN) + immediate 911 call; multidisciplinary CODE STROKE team activation within 10 minutes
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognition + time-critical activation. Each minute matters (~1.9 million neurons lost per minute). Foundation for treatment. RN role: rapid assessment + team activation + family + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: BP 192/108 on arrival requires lowering BEFORE tPA (must be <185/110); LABETALOL 10 mg IV bolus (alternative nicardipine drip + clevidipine + hydralazine + enalaprilat); avoid rapid drops (>20% MAP reduction extends penumbra into infarct); post-tPA target <180/105 × 24 hr to reduce hemorrhagic transformation risk; reassess q15 min during titration
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — BP management for tPA eligibility + safety. Standard practice. RN role: BP monitoring + medication administration + escalation.

opts.text: STAT IMAGING — CT HEAD non-contrast IMMEDIATELY (rule out hemorrhage + assess early ischemic changes ASPECTS 8/10 favorable); CTA HEAD + NECK (LARGE VESSEL OCCLUSION LEFT M1 — thrombectomy candidate); CT PERFUSION (core 30 mL + penumbra 180 mL = favorable for reperfusion + thrombectomy benefit; extended-window 6-24 hr per DAWN/DEFUSE-3 if needed); MRI selected cases
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — STAT neuroimaging + treatment selection. Standard AHA protocol. RN role: imaging coordination + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: tPA CONTRAINDICATIONS reviewed — no acute hemorrhage + no recent surgery + no recent stroke + no brain tumor + no bleeding diathesis + INR 1.1 normal + platelets 220 normal + not on anticoagulant + BP controlled <185/110 + glucose 142 acceptable + age 74 (no upper limit per AHA); ELIGIBLE; consent obtained from wife (proxy) + patient with limited communication
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — tPA contraindications systematic review. Standard practice. RN role: contraindication review + medication administration + family communication.

opts.text: IV ALTEPLASE (tPA) 0.9 mg/kg × 80 kg = 72 mg = 10% IV BOLUS 7.2 mg over 1 min + 90% INFUSION 64.8 mg over 60 min; DOOR-TO-NEEDLE TIME 40 minutes (within ≤60 min AHA goal); TENECTEPLASE 0.25 mg/kg IV bolus alternative emerging as comparable or superior + simpler administration (EXTEND-IA TNK + multiple trials); BRIDGING therapy = tPA + thrombectomy combined
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — tPA administration + dosing + Door-to-Needle. Standard AHA protocol. RN role: medication preparation + administration + monitoring.

opts.text: ENDOVASCULAR THROMBECTOMY for LVO within 24 hr per AHA + DAWN/DEFUSE-3; Door-to-Puncture ≤90 min goal; TICI 2b/3 recanalization successful; stent retriever (Solitaire) + aspiration catheter; general anesthesia preferred; complications include hemorrhagic transformation + vessel dissection + groin hematoma + contrast nephropathy; significantly improves outcomes vs tPA alone for LVO
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — endovascular thrombectomy + LVO management. Standard practice + significant outcome benefit. RN role: pre/post-procedure care + family + monitoring + escalation.

opts.text: POST-tPA + POST-THROMBECTOMY ICU CARE × 24 HR: continuous monitoring + neurochecks q15 min × 2 hr → q30 min × 6 hr → q1h × 16 hr; BP target <180/105; HOLD antiplatelets/anticoagulants × 24 hr; DYSPHAGIA SCREENING BEFORE ANY ORAL INTAKE (NPO until SLP swallow evaluation); DVT prophylaxis mechanical SCDs initially + heparin SC when bleeding risk acceptable; stress ulcer prophylaxis; skin + HOB ≥30° + oral care; family + chaplain + multidisciplinary
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — post-tPA ICU care + comprehensive bundle. Standard AHA protocol. RN role: monitoring + escalation + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: SECONDARY STROKE PREVENTION — APIXABAN 5 mg PO BID (DOAC for AFib — preferred over warfarin per AHA + ARISTOTLE trial; reduces stroke 50%+); ATORVASTATIN 80 mg PO daily (high-intensity statin per SPARCL trial); LISINOPRIL 10 mg PO daily (BP optimization); ASA 81 mg transitional then apixaban alone (cardioembolic stroke — no DAPT in this patient); BP target <140; diabetes management + lifestyle + smoking cessation + carotid evaluation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — secondary stroke prevention. Standard practice. RN role: medication administration + education + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY POST-STROKE CARE: PT (physical therapy + gait + balance + strength + mobility) + OT (ADLs + cognition + visual + perceptual) + SPEECH (language + swallowing + cognitive) + NEUROPSYCHOLOGY + RECREATIONAL/VOCATIONAL + CASE MANAGEMENT + SOCIAL WORK + CHAPLAIN + family + REHABILITATION specialized + DRIVING evaluation + lifelong management
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multidisciplinary rehab + lifelong management. Foundation for sustained recovery + return to function. RN role: coordination + advocacy + family + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation. No medication contraindications in current regimen. Not the priority cue but important for safety.

opts.text: ADMINISTER IV ALTEPLASE (tPA) IMMEDIATELY at NIHSS recognition WITHOUT obtaining CT head first — every minute matters + delaying for imaging wastes critical time
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS approach. CT HEAD NON-CONTRAST IS MANDATORY BEFORE tPA to RULE OUT INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE (absolute contraindication to tPA; tPA + ICH = life-threatening worsening + hemorrhage extension + death). Standard AHA protocol = STAT CT head + interpret + then tPA decision if criteria met. Skipping imaging is unacceptable + standard of care violation. RN role: ensure imaging occurs + standard pathway.

opts.text: Continue daily aspirin 81 mg post-tPA — antiplatelet provides additional stroke prevention + reduces thrombotic risk during acute period
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. HOLD ALL ANTIPLATELETS/ANTICOAGULANTS × 24 HR POST-tPA — adding antiplatelet during acute post-tPA period dramatically INCREASES hemorrhagic transformation risk (~5-10% baseline; antiplatelet/anticoagulant addition during this window increases substantially); STANDARD = NO antiplatelets/anticoagulants × 24 hr post-tPA + repeat CT head clear before resuming/initiating + then start secondary prevention Day 2. AHA protocol.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What stroke + tPA + thrombectomy + post-stroke principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: ACUTE ISCHEMIC STROKE = time-critical neurological emergency (each minute ~1.9 million neurons lost); CODE STROKE TEAM ACTIVATION within 10 min of recognition + multidisciplinary; NIH STROKE SCALE 0-42 standardized assessment + severity classification; STAT NEUROIMAGING (CT head non-contrast first + CTA head/neck + CT perfusion for extended-window selection) per AHA guidelines
opts.rat: CORRECT — stroke recognition + activation + imaging framework. Standard practice. Foundation for time-critical management.

opts.text: IV ALTEPLASE (tPA) criteria per AHA: ischemic stroke + measurable deficit + within 4.5 HR LKW + age 18+ + BP <185/110 + no hemorrhage + INR <1.7 + platelets >100k + no recent surgery/stroke/bleed/anticoagulation + multiple specific contraindications; DOSE 0.9 mg/kg max 90 mg = 10% bolus + 90% infusion 60 min; TENECTEPLASE 0.25 mg/kg single bolus alternative emerging as comparable or superior
opts.rat: CORRECT — tPA criteria + dosing + alternatives framework. Standard practice. RN role: contraindication review + medication administration.

opts.text: BP MANAGEMENT for tPA: pre-tPA target <185/110 (must be controlled before initiation; sustained higher pressures contraindicate); LABETALOL 10-20 mg IV bolus + nicardipine 5 mg/hr drip + clevidipine + hydralazine + enalaprilat options; AVOID rapid drops; post-tPA target <180/105 × 24 hr to reduce hemorrhagic transformation risk; transition to standard goals after 24 hr
opts.rat: CORRECT — BP management framework. Standard practice. Foundation for tPA eligibility + safety.

opts.text: ENDOVASCULAR THROMBECTOMY for LVO per AHA + DAWN/DEFUSE-3 trials: anterior circulation LVO + NIHSS ≥6 + ASPECTS ≥6 + Door-to-Puncture ≤90 min; EXTENDED WINDOW 6-24 hr with favorable perfusion imaging; bridging therapy (tPA + thrombectomy) common; TICI 2b/3 successful reperfusion + significantly improves outcomes vs tPA alone (50-60% mRS 0-2 vs 25-30%)
opts.rat: CORRECT — thrombectomy framework + extended window. Standard practice. RN role: pre/post-procedure care + monitoring + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: POST-tPA ICU CARE × 24 HR: continuous monitoring + neurochecks q15 min × 2 hr → q30 min × 6 hr → q1h × 16 hr; BP target <180/105; HOLD antiplatelets/anticoagulants × 24 hr (5-10% hemorrhagic transformation risk; 5% symptomatic ICH); repeat CT head 24 hr post-tPA before resuming antithrombotic; multidisciplinary post-stroke care begins
opts.rat: CORRECT — post-tPA ICU care framework. Standard AHA protocol. Foundation for safety + outcome.

opts.text: DYSPHAGIA SCREENING BEFORE ANY ORAL INTAKE — NPO until SLP swallow evaluation passed; aspiration pneumonia common post-stroke + preventable; bedside swallow screen by RN initial (water swallow test) + SLP formal evaluation + gradual diet advancement per evaluation (NPO → ice chips → puree → soft → regular) + modified textures + thickened liquids if needed; nutrition support; HOB ≥30° + supervised feeding + suction
opts.rat: CORRECT — dysphagia framework + aspiration prevention. Standard practice. Foundation for nutrition + safety + complications prevention.

opts.text: SECONDARY STROKE PREVENTION per AHA based on etiology: NON-CARDIOEMBOLIC (small vessel + atherosclerotic) = ASPIRIN + DAPT 90 days (POINT + CHANCE for minor stroke/TIA) then aspirin alone + statin high-intensity + BP + lifestyle + carotid intervention if indicated; CARDIOEMBOLIC (AFib + valvular) = ANTICOAGULATION (DOAC preferred over warfarin per AHA + ARISTOTLE/RE-LY/ROCKET-AF — apixaban + dabigatran + rivaroxaban + edoxaban; reduces stroke 50%+ + ICH less than warfarin) + statin + BP + lifestyle
opts.rat: CORRECT — secondary prevention framework based on etiology. Standard practice. RN role: medication administration + education + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY POST-STROKE CARE: PT + OT + SPEECH + NEUROPSYCHOLOGY + RECREATIONAL/VOCATIONAL + CASE MANAGEMENT + SOCIAL WORK + CHAPLAIN + family + REHABILITATION specialized (acute inpatient + SNF + outpatient + home) + DRIVING evaluation + EMPLOYMENT + LIFELONG management + CULTURAL sensitivity + family integration
opts.rat: CORRECT — multidisciplinary rehab + lifelong management framework. Foundation for sustained recovery + return to function.

opts.text: Administer IV ALTEPLASE (tPA) WITHOUT obtaining CT head first to maximize neuroprotection — every minute matters + delaying for imaging wastes critical time
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. CT HEAD NON-CONTRAST IS MANDATORY BEFORE tPA to RULE OUT INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE (absolute contraindication). Standard AHA protocol = STAT CT head + interpret + then tPA decision if criteria met. Skipping unacceptable.

opts.text: Continue daily aspirin 81 mg + apixaban post-tPA + DAPT initiation immediately to maximize secondary stroke prevention
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. HOLD ALL ANTIPLATELETS/ANTICOAGULANTS × 24 HR POST-tPA — adding antiplatelet/anticoagulant during acute post-tPA period dramatically INCREASES hemorrhagic transformation risk. Standard practice = no antiplatelets/anticoagulants × 24 hr post-tPA + repeat CT clear + then start secondary prevention Day 2.

opts.text: WAIT 24 HOURS to assess swallow function before any treatment — aspiration is unlikely in alert patients + early assessment is too premature
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. DYSPHAGIA SCREENING BEFORE ANY ORAL INTAKE is standard post-stroke care because aspiration risk is HIGH even in alert patients (silent aspiration common). NPO until bedside swallow screen + SLP formal evaluation. Aspiration pneumonia is common post-stroke complication + preventable with appropriate dysphagia management. Standard practice + reduces mortality.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns at this acute stroke presentation
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: CODE STROKE TEAM ACTIVATION + STAT IMAGING + tPA DECISION + Door-to-Needle ≤60 min r/t NIHSS 18 + within 4.5 hr LKW + BE FAST recognition + LVO likely; ABC + IV access + STAT labs + STAT CT head/CTA/CTP + BP control + tPA contraindication review + IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg administration
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Stroke is time-critical (~1.9 million neurons lost/min). CODE STROKE + tPA decision within ≤60 min foundation for outcome. RN role: rapid response + team activation + STAT workflow.

opts.text: ENDOVASCULAR THROMBECTOMY for LVO + Door-to-Puncture ≤90 min r/t left M1 occlusion confirmed on CTA + favorable CTP mismatch + thrombectomy candidate; bridging therapy with tPA + thrombectomy; TICI 2b/3 recanalization successful; significant outcome improvement vs tPA alone
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Thrombectomy significantly improves outcomes for LVO. Time-critical. Multidisciplinary. RN role: pre/post-procedure care + monitoring + family communication.

opts.text: POST-tPA + POST-THROMBECTOMY ICU CARE × 24 HR r/t continuous monitoring + neurochecks q15 min × 2 hr → q30 min × 6 hr → q1h × 16 hr; BP target <180/105; HOLD antiplatelets/anticoagulants × 24 hr; DYSPHAGIA SCREENING BEFORE ANY ORAL INTAKE (NPO until SLP evaluation); DVT prophylaxis mechanical SCDs; stress ulcer prophylaxis; skin + HOB ≥30° + multidisciplinary
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Post-tPA ICU care + comprehensive bundle. Standard AHA protocol. Foundation for safety + outcome + complications prevention.

opts.text: SECONDARY STROKE PREVENTION + AFib Management r/t new-onset AFib + cardioembolic stroke; APIXABAN 5 mg PO BID (DOAC for AFib — preferred over warfarin per AHA + ARISTOTLE trial; reduces stroke 50%+); ATORVASTATIN 80 mg PO daily (high-intensity); LISINOPRIL 10 mg PO daily (BP optimization); diabetes management + lifestyle + smoking cessation + cardiac echo for AFib + cardioembolic source + cardiology consultation
opts.rat: #4 — Significant + ongoing. Secondary prevention foundation for sustained outcome + recurrence prevention. RN role: medication administration + education + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: Multidisciplinary Post-Stroke Care + Rehabilitation + Family-Centered Care r/t PT + OT + speech + neuropsychology + case management + social worker + chaplain (Catholic family) + rehabilitation specialized (acute inpatient + SNF + outpatient + home) + driving evaluation + cultural sensitivity + family integration + cognitive recovery + depression screening + community + lifelong management
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + at discharge. Comprehensive multidisciplinary rehab + lifelong management + family + community. Foundation for sustained recovery + return to function.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this patient.

opts.text: CODE STROKE TEAM ACTIVATION + multidisciplinary engagement + STAT imaging (CT head non-contrast + CTA + CT perfusion) + parallel processing + Door-to-Needle ≤60 min goal + Door-to-Puncture ≤90 min goal
opts.rat: Standard AHA Code Stroke protocol. Time-critical. Multidisciplinary. RN role: team activation + STAT workflow + scribe.

opts.text: BP CONTROL with LABETALOL 10-20 mg IV bolus q10 min (alternative nicardipine drip 5 mg/hr + clevidipine + hydralazine + enalaprilat) for pre-tPA target <185/110 + post-tPA target <180/105 × 24 hr; avoid rapid drops; arterial line for continuous monitoring
opts.rat: Standard BP management for tPA. Standard practice. RN role: BP monitoring + medication administration + escalation.

opts.text: IV ALTEPLASE (tPA) 0.9 mg/kg max 90 mg = 10% bolus + 90% infusion over 60 min (for 80 kg patient = 7.2 mg bolus + 64.8 mg infusion); within 4.5 hr LKW + all criteria met + contraindications excluded + consent obtained
opts.rat: Standard tPA administration. Time-critical. RN role: medication preparation + administration + monitoring.

opts.text: ENDOVASCULAR THROMBECTOMY for LVO (left M1) + favorable imaging + Door-to-Puncture ≤90 min; bridging therapy with tPA; stent retriever + aspiration catheter; general anesthesia + neurointerventionalist + interventional radiology suite; TICI 2b/3 reperfusion target
opts.rat: Standard thrombectomy for LVO. Significant outcome improvement. RN role: pre/post-procedure care + monitoring + family.

opts.text: POST-tPA ICU CARE × 24 HR: continuous cardiac monitoring + arterial line + IV access + Foley + neurochecks q15 min × 2 hr → q30 min × 6 hr → q1h × 16 hr; BP target <180/105; HOLD antiplatelets/anticoagulants × 24 hr; DYSPHAGIA SCREENING BEFORE ORAL INTAKE; DVT prophylaxis mechanical SCDs; stress ulcer prophylaxis; skin + HOB ≥30°; family + chaplain
opts.rat: Standard post-tPA ICU care + comprehensive bundle. Standard AHA protocol. Foundation for safety + outcome.

opts.text: DYSPHAGIA SCREENING BEFORE ANY ORAL INTAKE — NPO until bedside swallow screen by RN + SLP formal evaluation; gradual diet advancement (NPO → ice chips → puree → soft → regular) + modified textures + thickened liquids if needed; nutrition support; aspiration precautions HOB ≥30° + supervised feeding + suction
opts.rat: Standard dysphagia screening + aspiration prevention. Foundation for nutrition + safety + pneumonia prevention.

opts.text: SECONDARY PREVENTION starting Day 2 post-tPA if CT clear: APIXABAN 5 mg PO BID (DOAC for AFib — preferred over warfarin per AHA + ARISTOTLE); ATORVASTATIN 80 mg PO daily (high-intensity); LISINOPRIL 10 mg PO daily (BP); diabetes + lifestyle + cardiac echo for AFib source + cardiology consultation
opts.rat: Standard secondary prevention. Foundation for recurrence prevention. Standard practice.

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY ENGAGEMENT: neurology + neurointerventionalist + ED + stroke RN + ICU intensivist + pharmacy + lab + radiology + PT + OT + speech + neuropsychology + case management + social worker + chaplain (Catholic) + cardiology (AFib) + PCP medical home; family-centered + cultural sensitivity + transparent communication + lifelong management
opts.rat: Standard multidisciplinary care. Foundation for sustained recovery + return to function.

opts.text: Administer IV ALTEPLASE (tPA) WITHOUT obtaining CT head first to maximize neuroprotection — every minute matters + delaying for imaging wastes critical time
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. CT HEAD MANDATORY BEFORE tPA to rule out intracranial hemorrhage (absolute contraindication). Standard AHA protocol = STAT CT head + then tPA. Skipping unacceptable + standard of care violation.

opts.text: Continue daily aspirin 81 mg + start apixaban immediately post-tPA + DAPT to maximize secondary stroke prevention
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. HOLD ALL ANTIPLATELETS/ANTICOAGULANTS × 24 HR POST-tPA — adding during acute post-tPA period dramatically INCREASES hemorrhagic transformation risk. Standard practice = no antiplatelets/anticoagulants × 24 hr + repeat CT clear + then start Day 2.

opts.text: DELAY dysphagia screening for 24 hours — aspiration unlikely in alert patients + early assessment premature; allow patient to drink water and eat normally during initial 24 hours
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. DYSPHAGIA SCREENING BEFORE ANY ORAL INTAKE is standard post-stroke care — aspiration risk HIGH even in alert patients (silent aspiration common). NPO until bedside swallow screen + SLP formal evaluation. Standard practice + reduces aspiration pneumonia + mortality.

opts.text: Initiate aggressive BP lowering to SBP <120 to maximize cerebral perfusion + prevent further infarction
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. Aggressive BP lowering (>20% MAP reduction) extends penumbra into infarct + worsens stroke. STANDARD = pre-tPA <185/110 + post-tPA <180/105 × 24 hr + after 24 hr standard goals + gentle lowering. Maintaining adequate cerebral perfusion pressure essential.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: FIRST action sequence at acute stroke presentation
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: ACTIVATE CODE STROKE TEAM within 10 minutes of recognition (pre-arrival from EMS or at ED triage); ABC + IV access × 2 large-bore + cardiac monitor + pulse ox + STAT labs (CBC + BMP + LFT + lipase + lactate + ABG + coags + glucose + lipids + HbA1c) + NIHSS assessment by RN/stroke team
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Team activation + initial assessment + STAT workup. Time-critical foundation.

opts.text: STAT IMAGING: CT HEAD non-contrast (rule out hemorrhage + assess early ischemic changes ASPECTS); CTA HEAD + NECK (assess LVO); CT PERFUSION (extended-window selection + favorable mismatch); MRI selected cases; PARALLEL processing during BP control + labs + tPA preparation
opts.rat: #2 — Second. STAT imaging + parallel processing. Standard AHA protocol. Foundation for treatment selection.

opts.text: BP CONTROL with LABETALOL 10-20 mg IV bolus (alternative nicardipine drip + clevidipine + hydralazine) for pre-tPA target <185/110; avoid rapid drops; arterial line for continuous monitoring; reassess q15 min during titration
opts.rat: #3 — Third. BP management for tPA eligibility. Time-critical. RN role: medication administration + monitoring + escalation.

opts.text: ADMINISTER IV ALTEPLASE (tPA) 0.9 mg/kg max 90 mg = 10% bolus + 90% infusion 60 min (after CT clear + criteria met + contraindications excluded + consent + BP controlled); Door-to-Needle ≤60 min goal; ENDOVASCULAR THROMBECTOMY for LVO within Door-to-Puncture ≤90 min (bridging therapy with tPA)
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Definitive treatment + Door-to-Needle goal + bridging therapy. Standard practice. RN role: medication preparation + administration.

opts.text: POST-tPA ICU CARE × 24 HR + comprehensive bundle: continuous monitoring + neurochecks per protocol + BP <180/105 + HOLD antiplatelets/anticoagulants × 24 hr + DYSPHAGIA SCREENING before any oral intake + DVT prophylaxis mechanical + skin + HOB 30° + multidisciplinary engagement + SECONDARY PREVENTION Day 2 + DISCHARGE planning + REHABILITATION + lifelong management + family + chaplain + cultural sensitivity
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing + foundational. Post-tPA care + comprehensive bundle + secondary prevention + multidisciplinary lifelong. Foundation for sustained recovery.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: ED 0900 (Door-to-Needle 40 min): tPA initiated + Door-to-Needle within ≤60 min goal; BP controlled to <185/110; tPA contraindications excluded; bridging thrombectomy activated; multidisciplinary team coordinated; family at bedside
opts.rat: Standard expected response — tPA initiated within target time + multidisciplinary engagement. Foundation for ongoing care + thrombectomy.

opts.text: Thrombectomy 1000-1100: TICI 2b/3 recanalization successful; large M1 clot retrieved + small thrombus extending to M2; no procedural complications; femoral site secured; transfer to neurology ICU
opts.rat: Standard expected response — successful reperfusion. Foundation for clinical improvement + outcomes.

opts.text: Neurology ICU 1200 (1 hr post-thrombectomy): NIHSS DRAMATICALLY IMPROVED from 18 → 6 (R arm 4/5 from 0/5 + R leg 4/5 from 0/5 + speech improving + facial droop mild + alert + follows commands); CT head post-procedure shows reperfusion + no hemorrhagic transformation; family relieved + grateful
opts.rat: DRAMATIC clinical response to tPA + thrombectomy = excellent reperfusion + favorable outcome trajectory. Standard ideal response. Foundation for continued recovery.

opts.text: Day 2 morning 0700 (24 hr post-tPA): NIHSS 4 (continuing improvement); CT head 24 hr post-tPA NO hemorrhagic transformation; DYSPHAGIA SCREENING by SLP PASSED with mild residue + safe for soft diet with thin liquids; SECONDARY PREVENTION started — apixaban 5 mg BID + atorvastatin 80 mg + lisinopril 10 mg; PT/OT/SPEECH initiated; family supportive + grateful
opts.rat: Standard expected Day 2 trajectory + DKA resolution + secondary prevention + multidisciplinary engagement. Foundation for ongoing recovery + transition.

opts.text: Day 1 ALTERNATIVE — patient developed acute neurologic deterioration with new severe headache + N/V + Cushing triad (HR 50 + BP 180/110 + irregular respirations) + worsening LOC; emergent CT head reveals SYMPTOMATIC INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE (sICH) post-tPA; STOP tPA infusion + emergent neurosurgery consult + FFP + cryoprecipitate + reverse antifibrinolytic + ICU management + family meeting
opts.rat: ⚠️ SYMPTOMATIC INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE (sICH) is recognized complication of tPA (~5% incidence). Standard response = STOP tPA + reverse + emergent neurosurgery + ICU + family + multidisciplinary. RN role: recognize early + escalate + medication + family communication.

opts.text: Day 3 — patient stable + transitioning from ICU to stroke unit floor; NIHSS 3 (continued improvement); continuing PT/OT/SPEECH + improving + walking with assistance + speech improving + family supportive + chaplain ongoing + Catholic family rituals; cardiology consult + TTE shows AFib without structural cause + apixaban continued + cardiology outpatient
opts.rat: Standard expected Day 3 trajectory + transition + continued improvement + multidisciplinary engagement + family. Foundation for transfer to rehab.

opts.text: Day 7 — acute inpatient rehabilitation transfer + intensive PT/OT/SPEECH + improving function + walking with cane + speech functional + dysphagia resolved + reading + family visiting + grandchildren visits + outpatient stroke follow-up scheduled + driving evaluation when ready + community reintegration planning
opts.rat: Standard expected transfer to acute inpatient rehabilitation + multidisciplinary engagement + family + community + return to function planning. Foundation for sustained recovery.

opts.text: Month 3 outpatient: SUSTAINED RECOVERY × 3 months + minimal residual deficits (mild dysarthria + occasional word-finding difficulty + functional independence + driving cleared + returned to community activities + bridge club + church + family activities); apixaban continued + atorvastatin + lisinopril + metformin + lifestyle + Mediterranean diet + multidisciplinary outpatient + neurology + cardiology + PCP
opts.rat: Excellent 3-month outcome + sustained recovery + functional independence + return to community + multidisciplinary outpatient + secondary prevention. Standard ideal trajectory.

opts.text: 1-year follow-up: NO recurrent stroke + sustained AFib management with apixaban + AFib well-controlled + HbA1c 7.2 + BP 128/76 + LDL 65 + sustained recovery + community engaged + family supportive + cultural + spiritual; grandchildren engaged + family thriving; future planning + retirement enjoyment + memorable life events
opts.rat: Excellent 1-year outcome + sustained recovery + no recurrence + comprehensive secondary prevention + family + community + meaningful life. Standard ideal long-term trajectory.

opts.text: Day 1 ALTERNATIVE — RN administered IV alteplase (tPA) WITHOUT obtaining CT head first based on clinical stroke recognition + 'every minute matters' rationale; CT head obtained 1 hr later showing PREVIOUSLY UNRECOGNIZED INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE → patient developed massive hemorrhage extension + multi-organ failure + DEATH; sentinel event + RCA + just culture + system improvement + protocol education
opts.rat: ⚠️ FATAL MEDICATION SAFETY EVENT — administered tPA without mandatory pre-tPA CT head to rule out hemorrhage. AHA protocol violated. CT HEAD MANDATORY before tPA. RCA + just culture + system improvement + protocol education. This scenario demonstrates WHY mandatory imaging matters. NEVER skip CT before tPA.
